description: The displayed image is a low-resolution software interface button marked with the text "Check for updates", paired with a logo of a red bookmark inside an incomplete ring composed of blue and yellow segments. This image has no correlation with the provided paper studying the plastic flow behavior of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during 250℃ warm multiaxial forging. No valid material science related information including microstructure features, mechanical property data, model simulation results, or processing routes can be extracted from this image. It is inferred that this is an incorrectly uploaded or failed loaded image instead of the target academic figure of the paper.

This figure is a schematic diagram of the multiaxial forging (MAF) process for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel samples. The original cuboid sample has an initial dimension of 20×20×30 mm. The schematic illustrates the full procedure of one MAF pass, including three sequential compression steps along three orthogonal directions, each followed by a 90° rotation around a specified axis (y, z, then x axis in sequence), with 33% height reduction per compression step. The pressing direction, constrained direction, and material flow direction during each compression are clearly marked. This schematic demonstrates the operation principle of warm MAF conducted at 250 °C in the study, supporting the experimental boundary condition setup for subsequent finite element simulation and modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model validation.

This image displays physical photographs of forged billets and machined tensile test specimens of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after different warm multiaxial forging (MAF) passes, placed above a metric ruler with centimeter scale for size reference. From left to right, the three groups correspond to samples after 1 MAF pass (MAF-1), 3 MAF passes (MAF-3), and 5 MAF passes (MAF-5) respectively; each group includes 1 forged billet on the top and 2 cut dog-bone shaped tensile specimens at the bottom. All samples are processed via warm MAF at 250 °C, with 33% normal strain per orthogonal compression direction per pass. The steel fractures on the 6th forging attempt, so 5 passes is the maximum achievable processing pass number. This figure confirms successful fabrication of MAF-processed steel samples for subsequent microstructure characterization and mechanical property testing, supporting investigation of plastic flow behavior during warm MAF of this low-density steel.

This figure presents two schematic diagrams for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF), all dimensions are in mm. Subfigure (a) illustrates the MAF-processed bulk sample with dimensions 30 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm, marking the flow direction, constrained direction, pressing direction, the characterized area for XRD and EBSD analysis, as well as the sampling positions for tensile test specimens. Subfigure (b) details the geometry of the miniature round tensile specimen: total length 26.5 mm, gauge length 16 mm, transition fillet R1, gauge diameter 4 mm, grip end diameter 7.2 mm. This schematic defines the exact sampling positions for microstructural characterization and mechanical testing, ensuring the tested properties are correlated with microstructures at the same location, which supports the study on microstructure evolution during MAF and validation of the modified Johnson-Cook plastic flow model.

This figure presents half-sectional views of 3D finite element models of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel workpiece inside a closed die before and after compression for warm multiaxial forging simulation. The left subplot shows the undeformed rectangular initial workpiece, while the right subplot displays the deformed workpiece after compression: the sample is compressed along the Y (height) direction by 10 mm, expands along the X (flow) direction, and shows no deformation along the Z direction due to die constraint. The finite element simulation is performed via ABAQUS/Explicit, with die and punch set as discrete rigid bodies, the workpiece as a deformable body meshed with 41382 C3D8R hexahedral elements of 0.6 mm size, and a friction coefficient of 0.15 applied at the workpiece-die interface. This figure validates that the finite element model can accurately reproduce the deformation characteristics of the multiaxial forging process, supporting subsequent flow stress, microstructure and mechanical property prediction combining the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model.

This figure shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after different warm multiaxial forging (MAF) passes. Four patterns correspond to samples MAF-0 (0 forging pass, initial solution treated state), MAF-1 (1 pass), MAF-3 (3 passes), and MAF-5 (5 passes), with x-axis as 2θ (degree, ranging 40 to 120°) and y-axis as diffraction intensity (count). For MAF-0, characteristic peaks of austenite (γ) phase (111), (200), (220), (311), (222) and minor κ(220) peak are identified. As MAF pass number increases, diffraction peak intensities gradually decrease while peak widths increase, confirming grain refinement and dislocation density elevation during MAF. All samples retain single austenite matrix without new carbide precipitations, indicating precipitation strengthening contribution remains constant during MAF. These results support the conclusion that strength increment during MAF is mainly attributed to dislocation accumulation and grain refinement.

This chart shows the effect of equivalent plastic strain on dislocation density of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed by 250℃ warm multiaxial forging. The black square markers represent experimentally measured dislocation density values, while the red dashed line denotes predicted values fitted using the equation ρ = 7.96E14 + k1*(1-exp(-k2*x)). The fitted parameters are k1 = 7.90516E15 ± 5.28171E14 m⁻², k2 = 1.81319 ± 0.42469, with an R-square (COD) value of 0.98554, indicating high goodness of fit. Dislocation density increases rapidly at low equivalent plastic strain, then its growth rate slows down and tends to saturate as strain increases, rising from ~7.96×10¹⁴ m⁻² at 0 strain to ~9×10¹⁵ m⁻² at equivalent strain of 2.3. The obtained fitting parameters are applied to the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model to predict the plastic flow behavior of the steel during warm multiaxial forging.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after different warm multiaxial forging (MAF) passes. Subfigures (a)-(d) are image quality maps for samples MAF-0 (0 pass), MAF-1 (1 pass), MAF-3 (3 passes), and MAF-5 (5 passes) respectively, with green lines marking low-angle grain boundaries (1°-15° misorientation) and red lines marking high-angle grain boundaries (15°-180° misorientation). The high-angle grain boundary fraction decreases from 0.622 (MAF-0) to 0.240 (MAF-3) then increases to 0.699 (MAF-5), while the low-angle boundary fraction shows the opposite trend. Subfigures (e)-(h) are normal direction (pressing direction) inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for the same four samples, with all scale bars equal to 60 μm. The initial MAF-0 sample has strong texture, which is gradually randomized with increasing MAF passes, accompanied by obvious grain refinement. This figure reveals the microstructural evolution law of grain boundary characteristics and texture changes during MAF, supporting the correlation between microstructure evolution and mechanical property variations of the test steel.

This figure consists of two subplots characterizing grain size evolution in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to 250℃ warm multiaxial forging (MAF) with 0, 1, 3, 5 passes (denoted MAF-0, MAF-1, MAF-3, MAF-5). Subplot (a) plots area fraction against grain size: as MAF pass count increases, the grain size distribution shifts to smaller values and becomes progressively narrower and more uniform, with peak area fraction decreasing for larger grains and increasing for finer grains, confirming progressive grain refinement during deformation. Subplot (b) shows the relationship between equivalent plastic strain and average grain size, with measured data points fitted by an exponential model (d = 50*exp(-0.63999*x)) that achieves an R-square of 0.99052, indicating excellent prediction accuracy. Average grain size reduces from 50 μm at 0 strain to ~13 μm at 2.3 equivalent strain (5 MAF passes). This figure validates the strain-dependent grain refinement law applied in the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model for predicting the steel’s plastic flow behavior during MAF.

This set of figures shows Electron Backscattered Diffraction (EBSD) derived Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed with different numbers of 250℃ warm multiaxial forging (MAF) passes. Subplot (a) corresponds to the as-solution treated sample (MAF-0, 0 forging passes) with an average KAM of 0.34°, dominated by blue color indicating very low initial local misorientation and dislocation density. Subplot (b) is for MAF-1 (1 forging pass, equivalent strain ~0.46) with average KAM of 0.72°, subplot (c) for MAF-3 (3 passes, equivalent strain ~1.4) with average KAM of 0.87°, and subplot (d) for MAF-5 (5 passes, equivalent strain ~2.3) with average KAM of 1.07°. All subplots share a 60 μm scale bar, with a uniform color scale ranging from 0° (blue, minimum misorientation) to 5° (red, maximum misorientation). The continuous rise in average KAM with increasing MAF passes demonstrates progressive accumulation of local lattice distortion and dislocation density as plastic strain increases, validates the simulated dislocation evolution trend during MAF, and supports the conclusion that dislocation accumulation and strain hardening are enhanced with more forging passes.

This figure presents engineering stress-strain curves of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after different warm multiaxial forging (MAF) passes, covering four samples: MAF-0 (0 forging passes, solution-treated initial sample), MAF-1 (1 MAF pass), MAF-3 (3 MAF passes), and MAF-5 (5 MAF passes). The x-axis records engineering strain, while the y-axis denotes engineering stress in MPa. A clear trend is observed: yield strength and ultimate tensile strength rise significantly with increasing MAF passes, while fracture elongation (ductility) decreases. Specifically, MAF-0 delivers a yield strength of ~380 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of ~762 MPa, and elongation of ~75%, while MAF-5 achieves a yield strength of ~1528 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of ~1548 MPa, with only ~8% elongation. Work hardening capacity drops sharply with more MAF passes, ascribed to increased dislocation density and grain refinement from MAF processing. This figure validates that warm MAF effectively strengthens the studied low-density steel via combined dislocation strengthening and grain boundary strengthening.

This figure presents the flow chart of the VUMAT (User Material Subroutine for Explicit Analysis) in ABAQUS, developed to implement the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel to simulate its plastic flow behavior during 250℃ warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The workflow starts with VUMAT initiation, followed by material constant calculation and step beginning. If step time equals 0, trial stress is calculated under pure elastic assumption; otherwise, deviatoric trial stress difference (sigdif) is computed. When sigdif > 0, stress is updated via the constitutive relation, otherwise equivalent plastic strain is set to zero. Then stress and 6 state-dependent variables (SDVs, including equivalent plastic strain, dislocation density, grain size, etc.) are updated, followed by updating of internal and dissipated inelastic energy before the program ends. This subroutine supports finite element simulation of the MAF process, with simulation results validated against experimental data of MAF-1, MAF-3 and MAF-5 samples.

This figure shows finite element simulation results of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging (MAF) at 250 ℃, with four subgraphs corresponding to different output parameters after 1, 3, 5 MAF passes. Subplot (a) presents equivalent strain maps, (b) shows dislocation density distribution maps (unit: m⁻²), (c) exhibits grain size distribution maps (unit: μm), and (d) displays flow stress maps (unit: MPa). Obvious trends are observed: as the number of MAF passes increases, equivalent strain, dislocation density, and flow stress rise consistently, with higher values concentrated in the central region of the forged sample, while grain size shows an inverse distribution, finer in the central area and coarser at the edge. These simulation results match experimental observations of dislocation accumulation, grain refinement, and strength enhancement during MAF, verifying that the modified Johnson-Cook model can effectively predict microstructural evolution and mechanical property changes during the warm MAF process.

This figure compares experimental and finite element (FE) simulated yield strength of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after different passes of 250℃ warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The x-axis represents the number of MAF passes (0, 1, 3, 5), and the y-axis denotes yield strength in MPa. The solid line with square markers represents experimentally measured yield strength, while the dashed line with circle markers represents FE simulation results obtained using a modified Johnson-Cook model that incorporates contributions from dislocation density and grain size. Both datasets show a continuous increase in yield strength with growing MAF passes: the initial yield strength at 0 passes is ~380 MPa, rising to ~1530 MPa after 5 passes for experimental samples. Simulation results match experimental values well for the first 3 passes, while simulated yield strength is slightly lower than the experimental value after 5 passes because the model neglects texture effects. This figure validates that the developed modified J-C model combined with FE simulation can effectively predict yield strength evolution of the studied steel during warm MAF.

This figure presents finite element simulated yield strength maps (unit: MPa) of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel after warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The color legend ranges from 699.4 MPa (dark blue) to 1358 MPa (dark red), showing yield strength distribution across different orthogonal sections of the forged sample. The simulation adopts a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model incorporating strain-dependent dislocation density and grain size contributions, which is validated against experimentally measured microstructural and mechanical data. A clear trend is observed that yield strength reaches the maximum value at the sample center, and gradually decreases towards the sample surface. The results confirm that the combination of the modified J-C model and finite element analysis can effectively predict the mechanical property evolution during MAF, with slight underestimation of strength after 5 MAF passes due to the neglect of texture effect in the simulation.
